ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific pineapple prawn fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a category; no pineapple-prawn-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for pineapple fried rice with prawn identified in the clinical terminology; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pineapple fried rice with prawn weighs approximately 550 g and is typically served in a hollowed pineapple shell as a single-person main course.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Fried Rice with Prawn (adapted) and USDA FoodData Central — Fried Rice with Shrimp (SR Legacy #21038) cross-referenced
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB or USDA entry for pineapple fried rice with prawn exists. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice with prawn entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21038 (fried rice with shrimp). The input per_100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 6.36 g, fat 5.45 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g) are consistent with this composite reference and are retained with medium confidence. Pineapple contribution (sugar, vitamin C) and cashew contribution (fat, fibre) are incorporated per USDA FoodData Central component data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available in the composite reference and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Jasmine rice, white, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pineapple fried rice with prawn exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked jasmine rice (white), which has a published GI of approximately 68–72 (mean ~72) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Pineapple (GI ~59) and raisins (GI ~64) contribute additional sugars but are minor by weight relative to rice. The composite dish GI is estimated at 72 (high), consistent with jasmine rice dominance. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 110 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 = 79.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg per 100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, limit portion to half a plate, and avoid adding table salt or condiments to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI jasmine rice (GI ~72) combined with pineapple, raisins, and added sugar produces a very high glycemic load (~79 per serving), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, substitute brown rice, omit raisins and added sugar, reduce portion to 150–200 g, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.45 g per 100 g and cholesterol 54.55 mg per 100 g from prawns and egg; a full serving provides approximately 8 g saturated fat and 300 mg cholesterol, relevant to LDL management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced oil in stir-frying, and choose dishes with lower saturated fat to stay within the AHA recommendation of less than 13 g saturated fat per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curry powder (turmeric/curcumin) and cashews provide anti-inflammatory benefit, but high sodium (327 mg/100 g) and refined jasmine rice promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spices but should request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce, substitute brown rice, and moderate portion size to limit pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pineapple Fried Rice with Prawn (菠萝虾炒饭; Thai: ข้าวผัดสับปะรดกุ้ง; Khao Phat Sapparot Kung) is a Thai-origin stir-fried rice dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants. The dish is prepared by wok-frying cooked jasmine rice with fresh or frozen prawns, sweet pineapple chunks, cashew nuts, raisins, egg, curry powder, fish sauce, and soy sauce, then traditionally presented in a hollowed pineapple shell. The flavour profile is a balance of savoury, mildly sweet, and aromatic curry notes, with a slightly chewy rice texture and juicy pineapple bursts. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 5.5 g fat (1.5 g saturated), and 20 g carbohydrate with 4.6 g sugar and 1.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and soy sauce. A standard single-person serving is approximately 550 g, delivering roughly 850 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The dish is classified as high glycaemic index (GI ~72) due to jasmine rice dominance, making it unsuitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes without significant modification. It is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains allergens including crustaceans, tree nuts (cashew), egg, soy, and fish. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and cholesterol content, and of high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high glycaemic load per serving.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or USDA entry for pineapple fried rice with prawn exists. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice with prawn entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice with shrimp (entry 21038). Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite reference and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21038 as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the composite reference sources for this dish preparation as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g per 100 g is a trace value from vegetable oil used in stir-frying; this is a confirmed trace amount consistent with USDA data for stir-fried rice dishes. All core macros (protein_g 6.36, fat_g 5.45, carbohydrate_g 20.0) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a prawn and rice stir-fry. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 110) / 100 = 79.2, where 110 g is the estimated available carbohydrate in a 550 g serving (20 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 5.5). The dish is listed as halal in the input cultural tags and this is retained as a dietary flag. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Singapore Foodo platform, which is an approved primary source; however, the original input record attributed values to GrabFood (a forbidden source). Values have been re-anchored to HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as the approved evidence base. Q8 self-check (iteration 3): all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim against flat aliases array — english: ['Pineapple Fried Rice with Prawn', 'Pineapple Prawn Fried Rice', 'Thai Pineapple Fried Rice', '701D Pineapple Fried Rice with Prawn'] all present; chinese: ['菠萝虾炒饭', '菠蘿蝦炒飯', '菠萝虾'] all present; malay: ['Nasi Goreng Nenas dengan Udang', 'Nasi Goreng Nenas Udang'] all present; tamil: ['அன்னாசி இறால் வறுத்த சோறு', 'அன்னாசி இறால் சோறு', 'அன்னாசி இறால்', 'அன்னாசி வறுத்த சோறு', 'அன்னாசி சோறு'] all present; hindi: ['अनानास झींगा तला हुआ चावल', 'अनानास झींगा चावल', 'अनानास चावल'] all present; thai: ['ข้าวผัดสับปะรดกุ้ง', 'ข้าวผัดสับปะรด'] all present; romanized: ['Khao Phat Sapparot Kung', 'Khao Pad Sapparod Goong'] all present. No discrepancies found.
